channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: Why OpenAI is selling seats, not software #chatgpt #ai #futureofwork
executive_summary: OpenAI is shifting its strategy by focusing on enterprise outcomes and compute allocation rather than just model quality as it heads into 2026. This approach is likened to running an airline with limited seats, emphasizing the importance of how AI is used in businesses rather than just who has the best technology. As the landscape evolves, understanding these dynamics will be crucial for success in the AI field.
bullets: OpenAI is operating 'like an airline with scarce seats,' meaning they have limited resources to allocate to users. This scarcity forces them to prioritize enterprise clients who can generate significant outcomes, similar to how an airline prioritizes first-class passengers for maximum profit.

Compute allocation is the real constraint for OpenAI, not just the quality of their models. This is like trying to fill a stadium with fans; it doesn't matter how good the band is if there aren't enough seats available for everyone who wants to attend.

Two-thirds of users do not understand AI capabilities, which creates a gap in effective utilization. This is akin to having a high-tech smartphone but only using it to make calls, missing out on its full potential.

OpenAI's rollback to faster, cheaper models signals a shift towards practicality over perfection. Think of it like a restaurant that decides to serve comfort food instead of gourmet dishes to attract more customers during tough economic times.

By 2026, OpenAI is focusing on two major areas: science and enterprise inference. This is like a company deciding to invest in both research and customer service to ensure long-term growth and immediate satisfaction.

Gemini's growth rate is at 30%, while ChatGPT's is only 5%. This disparity highlights how competition is evolving, similar to how a new sports team can quickly outpace an established one if they adopt better strategies.

Mental models do not stop at the office door, meaning that the way leaders think about AI impacts their entire organization. It's like a coach's mindset affecting the team's performance; if the coach is stuck in old ways, the team won't adapt to new plays.

The enterprise trap involves shallow usage of AI without proper delegation. This is similar to a manager who micromanages every task instead of empowering their team, leading to inefficiencies and burnout.

Leaders must focus on delegation and workflow ownership to thrive in the AI landscape. Just like a conductor who needs to trust their musicians to play their parts, leaders must empower their teams to utilize AI effectively.
Those comparing benchmarks will miss the structural shift happening in AI. It's like a driver who only looks at their speedometer instead of the road ahead; they may miss crucial turns and obstacles.

OpenAI's strategy emphasizes enterprise outcomes over just having the best model. This is like a chef who focuses on customer satisfaction rather than just perfecting their signature dish; the goal is to please the diners, not just impress them.

The focus on enterprise outcomes means that companies must adapt their workflows to integrate AI effectively. Think of it like a factory that needs to reconfigure its assembly line to accommodate new machinery for better efficiency.
key_takeaways: Prioritize understanding AI capabilities within your team. This is crucial because without this knowledge, your team may underutilize powerful tools, leading to wasted resources and missed opportunities.
By 2026, ensure your organization is investing in both science and enterprise inference. If you neglect this, you risk falling behind competitors who are adapting to the changing landscape of AI.
Set a rule that at least 50% of your AI projects must focus on delegation and workflow ownership. If you don't, you may end up with a team that is overwhelmed and unable to leverage AI effectively.

technical_terms.Compute Allocation: The distribution of computing resources to different tasks or users. Think of it like a chef deciding how much kitchen space to allocate for different dishes during a busy dinner service.
technical_terms.Enterprise Outcomes: The results or benefits that businesses achieve by implementing AI solutions. Like a farmer measuring the yield of crops after using new technology to improve growth.
technical_terms.Mental Models: The frameworks or beliefs that shape how individuals understand and interact with the world. Think of it like wearing glasses; the lenses you choose can change how clearly you see everything around you.
technical_terms.Delegation: The act of assigning responsibility to others to complete tasks. Like a captain of a ship who trusts their crew to handle different parts of the journey, allowing for smoother sailing.
technical_terms.Workflow Ownership: The responsibility for managing and optimizing processes within a team. It's like a project manager who oversees a construction site, ensuring each worker knows their role for the project to succeed.
technical_terms.Shallow Usage: Using a tool or technology in a limited way without fully leveraging its capabilities. This is like having a powerful car but only using it to drive around the block instead of taking it on a road trip.
technical_terms.Benchmarking: The process of comparing performance metrics against industry standards or competitors. Think of it like a runner checking their time against the best athletes to see how they measure up.
technical_terms.AI Capability: The potential functions and tasks that artificial intelligence can perform. Like a Swiss Army knife, which has multiple tools for different tasks, AI can handle various functions depending on how it's used.
technical_terms.Enterprise Trap: The situation where businesses use AI superficially without integrating it into their core operations. This is like a company that buys the latest technology but doesn't train employees on how to use it effectively.
technical_terms.Model Quality: The effectiveness and accuracy of an AI model in performing its tasks. Think of it like a recipe; the quality of the ingredients will determine how good the final dish tastes.
